eli3: Recommendation systems power Netflix, Spotify, Amazon, and TikTok — suggesting content or products users will enjoy. The goal: show the right item to the right user at the right time.
Two main approaches: collaborative filtering (users who bought X also bought Y) and content-based (if you like action movies, here's another). Modern systems combine both with deep learning.
At scale: Netflix has 200M users, 10k+ titles. Real-time personalization with sub-100ms latency while training on terabytes of interaction data daily.

whenWhy.title: Streaming media
whenWhy.body: Netflix, Spotify, YouTube: recommend what to watch/listen next. Increase engagement (hours watched), retention (reduce churn), and discovery (long-tail content). 80% of Netflix watches come from recommendations.

whenWhy.title: E-commerce
whenWhy.body: Amazon, Alibaba: 'Customers who bought X also bought Y', 'Recommended for you'. Increase basket size, cross-sell, upsell. Personalize homepage, emails, notifications. Direct revenue impact.

whenWhy.title: Social media
whenWhy.body: TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn: feed ranking. Balance engagement (clicks, likes) with diversity, freshness, and creator fairness. Real-time learning from interactions (dwell time > clicks).

whenWhy.title: Content platforms
whenWhy.body: Medium, Reddit, Pinterest: surface relevant content. Balance personalization with exploration (new topics). Editorial curation + algorithmic ranking. Community norms and safety filtering.

deepDiveIntro: Recommendation pipeline: (1) Candidate generation: from millions of items to hundreds. Matrix factorization, two-tower neural networks, approximate nearest neighbors (ANN). (2) Ranking: score candidates with heavy model. Deep neural networks with user/item features, context. (3) Re-ranking: diversity, freshness, business rules, exploration.

Algorithms: Collaborative filtering (user-item interactions matrix, SVD, ALS). Content-based (item features, TF-IDF, embeddings). Deep learning (two-tower, transformer-based sequential models). Reinforcement learning (bandits for exploration).

deepDive.heading: Candidate Generation
deepDive.body: Reduce millions to hundreds efficiently. Matrix factorization: latent factors for users/items. Two-tower: separate neural networks for user and item, dot product for similarity. ANN: index item embeddings, find nearest neighbors to user vector. FAISS (Facebook), ScaNN (Google), HNSW for fast search.

deepDive.heading: Deep Learning Ranking
deepDive.body: Wide & Deep: memorization (crossed features) + generalization (embeddings). DCN (Deep Cross Network): explicit feature crossing. DIN (Deep Interest Network): attention over user history. Transformer-based: model sequential interactions (SASRec, BERT4Rec). Features: user demographics, item categories, context (time, device), user history.

deepDive.heading: Real-Time Personalization
deepDive.body: Session-based recommendations: update within session based on actions. Online learning: model updates immediately from interactions. Feature freshness: items in cart, current browsing. Latency constraints: candidate generation cached, ranking real-time. A/B testing: measure CTR, conversion, session length, diversity.

deepDive.heading: Exploration vs Exploitation
deepDive.body: Exploitation: recommend items predicted to get highest engagement. Exploration: recommend new/unpopular items to learn preferences. Too much exploitation: filter bubble, boredom. Too much exploration: poor experience. Solutions: ε-greedy, Thompson Sampling, LinUCB bandit algorithms. Multi-armed bandits balance dynamically.

deepDive.heading: Scaling Challenges
deepDive.body: Precompute: candidate generation offline, store embeddings. Feature store: low-latency access to user/item features. Model serving: TensorFlow Serving, Triton for GPU inference. Fanout: fan out to all candidates in parallel. Caching: popular queries cached. Cold start: content-based for new items, popularity for new users.

takeaways: Collaborative filtering + content-based + deep learning combined.
Real-time: session-based updates, feature freshness, low latency.
Explore-exploit: bandit algorithms balance learning and engagement.
Scale: precomputation, ANN, feature stores, caching.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle the cold start problem?
edgeCases.a: New users: use onboarding (genre preferences), demographic similarity, popular items. New items: content-based features (category, tags), explore-exploit (show to get feedback), author/channel history. Cold start bandit: allocate exploration budget to new items. Hybrid: blend collaborative and content-based signals.

edgeCases.q: What about filter bubbles and diversity?
edgeCases.a: Problem: users only see similar content, miss discovery. Solutions: (1) MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance): diversity-aware re-ranking. (2) Category constraints: ensure mix of genres. (3) Exploration slots: dedicated positions for diverse items. (4) Serendipity metrics: measure unexpectedness of recommendations. (5) User control: 'Not interested', 'Why this?' transparency.

edgeCases.q: How do you measure recommendation success?
edgeCases.a: Online metrics: CTR, conversion rate, session length, retention, revenue. Offline metrics: precision@k, recall@k, NDCG, MAP, diversity, coverage. Correlation between offline and online metrics often weak. A/B testing essential. Long-term: cohort retention, lifetime value. Engagement ≠ satisfaction: high CTR on clickbait hurts long-term.

sources.label: Zhang et al. — Deep Learning based Recommender System: A Survey
